posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:
There once was a chap who posted here
Who would sometimes shed a tear
When seeing the word varietal abused
Knowing that it was variety that should be used
The Oxford Dictionary says varietal can be a noun
So does using varietal make one look like a clown
When referencing a grape instead of a wine
As we’ve all been prone to do at times
It’s my sincere desire to be varietally correct
Can it be varietal in lieu of variety, without the chap upset?

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: you have a varietal of options.

posts.name: Ed Kurtzman
posts.cooked: I don’t think I ever “corrected” anyone who said varietal when talking about a grape, but I’m not sure I’ve used the word varietal myself when talking about a wine. I definitely notice it when someone says varietal, but it’s kind of like when people talk about having tasted the '01 d’Yquem. There’s nothing absolutely wrong with what they said, it does say that on the label, and at least they’re really into wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: This is just a long-standing corruption, now accepted, deriving from the correct adjectival use in “varietal bottling”. I imagine some folks mistakenly imagined it was the varietal that was being bottled, and not the bottling that was varietal, and thus began using ‘varietal’ as a noun. The OED’s earliest citation for thus usage is 1955 so this has been going on some time, and it is probably a little late in the day to get pedantic about it, though I know some English grammar martinets who do, and it’s an expression I’d personally eschew.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

…and it is probably a little late in the day to get pedantic about it…

Never!

posts.name: Ethan H
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

This is just a long-standing corruption, now accepted, deriving from the correct adjectival use in “varietal bottling”. I imagine some folks mistakenly imagined it was the varietal that was being bottled, and not the bottling that was varietal, and thus began using ‘varietal’ as a noun. The OED’s earliest citation for thus usage is 1955 so this has been going on some time, and it is probably a little late in the day to get pedantic about it, though I know some English grammar martinets who do, and it’s an expression I’d personally eschew.

You are certainly correct that “variety” = a type of grape (nebbiolo, riesling, etc.), whereas “varietal” = a wine made from a single grape variety and marketed as such (or at least a wine containing a sufficient quantity of one variety, e.g. at least 75% in the USA, that it can be label-designated as such). Varieties should never be written with capital letters; whereas varietals may acceptably be. The marketing/label aspect is key. Yellowtail Shiraz is a “fighting varietal” but one would not refer to DRC Richebourg as a varietal even though it is 100% pinot noir, because it doesn’t say pinot noir on the label.

As for whether “varietal” is a corruption, I would disagree. This is what is known as a substantivized adjective, which English is full of (or for the prescriptivists out there: of which English is full).

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

This is just a long-standing corruption, now accepted, deriving from the correct adjectival use in “varietal bottling”. I imagine some folks mistakenly imagined it was the varietal that was being bottled, and not the bottling that was varietal, and thus began using ‘varietal’ as a noun. The OED’s earliest citation for thus usage is 1955 so this has been going on some time, and it is probably a little late in the day to get pedantic about it, though I know some English grammar martinets who do, and it’s an expression I’d personally eschew.

Indeed, “varietal” has long been accepted as a noun to refer to a wine made from a single variety of grape. That does not make it acceptable as a noun to refer to a variety of grape until we start referring to collies and cocker spaniels as varietals of canines. It is possible that this solecism has been in use long enough to get a citation in the OED as well. But it is soon enough so that if we vigilantly stamp it out, it may soon appear as an obsolete solecism. Otherwise, it will turn into a pointlessly divergent way to refer to grape varieties.

posts.name: Mike Officer
posts.cooked:
Ethan H:

Varieties should never be written with capital letters

Au contraire! When referring to a specific variety of any plant species, the variety is always capitalized. It’s Red Delicious, Granny Smith, Early Girl, Brandywine, Zinfandel, Pinot noir, etc. I know, many times these names are not capitalized. It’s just an indication that the writer/editor is unfamiliar with plant nomenclature.

posts.name: Ethan H
posts.cooked:
M Officer:
Ethan H:

Varieties should never be written with capital letters

Au contraire! When referring to a specific variety of any plant species, the variety is always capitalized. It’s Red Delicious, Granny Smith, Early Girl, Brandywine, Zinfandel, Pinot noir, etc. I know, many times these names are not capitalized. It’s just an indication that the writer/editor is unfamiliar with plant nomenclature.

Bah, what do you know about wine and wine grapes anyway?

Anyway, apparently we shouldn’t be calling them varieties at all but rather cultivars, and refer not to zinfandel or Zinfandel but rather to Vitis vinifera ‘Zinfandel’ etc.

However, instituting such a practice would require wineries either to bottle nothing smaller than magnums or else use 6-point fonts on their labels (to say nothing of their release letters, ahem). True, it might help the Americans challenge the Germans at their own game (I can just hear the ghost of Howard Cosell announcing the heavyweight title bout between 2013 Jos. Christoffel Jr. Ürziger Würzgarten Vitis vinifera ‘Riesling’ Trockenbeerenauslese *** Goldkapsel ‘Auction’ AP #6 and 2011 Carlisle Winery & Vineyards Russian River Valley “Dommen Ranch” Vitis vinfera ‘Grand Noir de Calmette’). However, I daresay anyone who tries that should be punished by having to drink a bottle of 1976 Inglenook Chablis. (Incidentally, ‘varietal’ apparently became substantivized in referring to wines as part of the effort to stop Americans from drinking California Chablis and California Burgundy.)

As for me, I will throw my hat in with the Associated Press stylebook and the New York Times and fight the good fight against Uppercasis ludicrosii by continuing to write (and drink – all good writers are drinkers) syrah, pinot noir, petit verdot, etc.

link_counts.title: ON LANGUAGE; WINES WITHOUT CAPS - The New York Times
When to Capitalize Animal and Plant Names

posts.name: Mike Officer
posts.cooked:
No worries Ethan. Feel free to make grape varieties lower case. I’ll stick to capitalizing them.

Cheers,
Mike

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Ethan H:

As for me, I will throw my hat in with the Associated Press stylebook and the New York Times and fight the good fight against Uppercasis ludicrosii by continuing to write (and drink – all good writers are drinkers) syrah, pinot noir, petit verdot, etc.

As an editor, I can tell you there’s no right or wrong on this. It just depends on what style rules you adhere to. Most news styles (AP, NYT, WSJ) aim for simplicity, so they disfavor caps in general, just as they mandate using numerals for numbers from 10 on up. Other publications have more formal styles. The New Yorker, for instance, uses the somewhat out-of-fashion serial comma (i.e., before “and” in a string) and spells out even percentage numbers (e.g., “sixty-nine percent”). I don’t know what The New Yorker does with grape types, but I’d lay a bet it would use capitals.

Happily, our mother tongue remains quite democratic and anarchic.

There might be an exception for names like Muller-Thurgau that derive quite recently from proper names. But I’m not sure.

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked:
The Times seems to be wobbly on this important topic, from a recipe on their site: ‘pippins, Gravensteins, Macintosh, Granny Smith, Pink Lady’.

If Jancis Robinson capitalizes and Ian d’Agata capitalizes then I do too.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
Since the Chicago Manual of Style doesn’t seem to have clearcut rule for the spelling of the names of cultivars but the International Code of Nomenclature for Cultivated Plants (ICNCP) does, I suggest we go by that. Based on the examples provided here, we should capitalize.

Another good reason for doing so, I think, is that grape varieties are individuals, cloned individuals, as opposed to species or subspecies.

link_counts.title: International Code of Nomenclature for Cultivated Plants - Wikipedia

posts.name: Richard Albert
posts.cooked: They call bread pizza in Chicago, instantly discrediting any authority, although easy to clearcut with a bread knife.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Oliver McCrum:

If Jancis Robinson capitalizes and Ian d’Agata capitalizes then I do too.

If anything, I would expect them to capitalise.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Anders Westholm:

Since the Chicago Manual of Style doesn’t seem to have clearcut rule for the spelling of the names of cultivars but the International Code of Nomenclature for Cultivated Plants (ICNCP) does, I suggest we go by that. Based on the examples provided here, we should capitalize.

Academic types tend to stick with more formal or archaic styles, so I don’t accept that as a precedent for general usage. Hell, many science types stick to Latin terms.

Is it right or wrong that British style is “Mr Hayes” and American style is “Mr. Hayes”? Or that periods/full stops go outside quotes in the UK and inside in the US (note British style of my previous sentence). Or that U.K. and U.S. are usually given periods.

If you’re losing sleep or getting hot under the collar about these things, don’t.

posts.name: Anders Westholm
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

Academic types tend to stick with more formal or archaic styles, so I don’t accept that as a precedent for general usage. Hell, many science types stick to Latin terms.

When the Chicago Manual of Style sticks to something, it usually does so based on some reasonable logic. And when Latin terms are still used in science, this is usually for good reason, such as in biology (including vine/wine biology) and medicine.

John Morris:

Or that periods/full stops go outside quotes in the UK and inside in the US (note British style of my previous sentence).

This is a good example of a case where one style, in this case the British, is preferable, as I see it, since it rests on a more reasonable logic. If you are quoting an entire sentence, the quote would of course go outside the full stop in the British case too but I realize that this is not the example you have in mind.

John Morris:

If you’re losing sleep or getting hot under the collar about these things, don’t.

As I thought you might guess from the emoticon at the end of my post, I don’t. Language develops as people see fit whether we like it or not. But as someone who, like you, spends significant parts of my working time writing, or reviewing the texts of others, I just like to make up my mind about them as far as my own practice is concerned.

Jay Miller:
William Kelley:

…and it is probably a little late in the day to get pedantic about it…

Never!

“Do not go gentle into that good night,
Rage, rage against the dying of the light.”

I blame the rampant misuse of ‘varietal’ when speaking of a grape variety on Robert Parker.
Without doubt he is the Offender-in-Chief.

posts.name: Corey Nøɤܝ©Ʞ
posts.cooked:
Robert.Fleming:

I blame the rampant misuse of ‘varietal’ when speaking of a grape variety on Robert Parker.
Without doubt he is the Offender-in-Chief.

Why not just split the difference and go with “varietally?”

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Corey N.:
Robert.Fleming:

I blame the rampant misuse of ‘varietal’ when speaking of a grape variety on Robert Parker.
Without doubt he is the Offender-in-Chief.

I just did

Why not just split the difference and go with “varietally?”
